unitTitle: Y9 Python programming with sequences of data (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: Warm up — selection and lists
lessons.objectives:
I can read a program and predict what it displays
I can use selection (if-elif-else) to choose what a program does
I can find and fix common syntax errors
I can create a list and read an item using its index
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: selection · condition · syntax error · list · index. TA: everyone has the Python editor (Trinket/Mu) open; pairs set with a driver and a navigator. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — “let me check” on the starter worksheet. Pupils MATCH each Python word (input/print/while/if/=) to what it does, then a short walkthrough question (no writing for Support).
   • Support: tick the one matching action.  • Core: explain operator precedence (+ before /).  • Challenge: why a condition needs a colon. Likely error: = vs == → fix-words “= stores a value, == checks if equal.”
2. (10–20) I-do → we-do (selection recap). Walk through the “How long until the weekend?” code (embedded screenshot): days are numbers 0–6; the colon and indent set each branch. Pairs predict the output on the activity worksheet.
3. (20–30) We-do — spot the syntax errors (card-sort on the activity worksheet): drag lines into “has a syntax error” vs “no syntax error”. TA: “prompt them to read the line aloud, do not fix it for them.”
4. (30–50) You-do — lists. Use the “months” list (embedded screenshot): items are numbered from 0.
   • Support: choose the index that gives a month.  • Core: fill the index gap; say what an index does.  • Challenge: write the weekday expression and fix `if day = 4:`. Keep a screenshot of the working program.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; each pair shows a list reading the right month. TA notes who can explain zero-based counting.
Differentiation is on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling — nobody’s screen looks “less done”. No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Playlist — operations on lists
lessons.objectives:
I can add and remove items from a list
I can find an item's index and count how often it appears
I can reorder a list with sort and reverse
I can choose the right list operation for a task
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: list · index · append · insert · pop · remove · index · count · sort · reverse. TA: editor open; pairs swap driver/navigator. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — “find the item” on the starter worksheet: recall that `planets[2]` is the THIRD planet (zero-based), then MATCH each operation (append/remove/index/count) to its job.
   • Support: first index is 0.  • Core: match the operations.  • Challenge: pop vs remove.
2. (5–15) I-do → we-do — operations on lists. Show dot notation: `planets.append("Mars")`. Card-sort on the activity worksheet: which operation adds, removes, finds/counts, or reorders.
3. (15–35) You-do — “the solar system”: add Uranus and Neptune to the end, then remove Pluto.
   • Support: pick the append line.  • Core: fill the method name; write the remove line. Likely error: `planets = append(...)` → fix-words “the list goes before the dot.” TA: prompt, don’t type for them.
4. (35–50) Challenge — dice battle (embedded dice screenshot): sort each player’s dice highest-first, then compare the top rolls. Keep a screenshot of the working program.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; name the operations used. TA notes who can explain which operation fits which job.
Differentiation on the SAME task; movement breaks are routine; no flashing/sound.

lessons.title: In a while, crocodile — looping with while
lessons.objectives:
I can use a while loop to repeat until a condition is met
I can build up a list inside a loop with append
I can use len and in on lists and strings
I can read a character of a string by its index
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: iteration · while · condition · len · in · index. TA: editor open; pairs swap roles. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — passwords on the starter worksheet: a real list of 1,000 leaked passwords (rude words removed). Recall `len` (how many), `in` (is mine there), `index` (where).
   • Support: what `in` checks.  • Core: what `index` returns.  • Challenge: how to warn the user. Keep it light and factual.
2. (10–25) I-do → we-do — “form a band”: live-code a while loop that keeps asking for instruments and `append`s them until `len(band) < 3` is no longer true. Indentation shows what is inside the loop.
3. (25–40) You-do — city hopping (embedded screenshot). Parsons task on the activity worksheet: order the jumbled lines so the program keeps picking random cities and adds them to the trip until it has five.
   • Support: choose the stopping condition.  • Core: fill the `append` gap; `"London"[0]`. Likely error: not appending inside the loop, so the trip never grows → fix-words “the loop body must add to the list.”
4. (40–52) Challenge — city guessing (embedded screenshot): a string is a list of characters, so `city[0]` is the first letter and `len(city)` counts the letters. Reveal the first-letter hint. Keep a screenshot.
5. (52–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; show a loop that built a list. TA notes who can say when the loop stops.
Differentiation on the SAME task; movement breaks are routine; no flashing/sound.

lessons.title: The famous for — looping over items
lessons.objectives:
I can use a for loop to go through a list
I can count items that match a condition
I can collect matching items into a new list
I can use a for loop over a string
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: iteration · for · item · count · collect. TA: editor open; pairs swap roles. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — shopping list on the starter worksheet: predict the output of `for item in shopping: print(item)`. The `for` syntax reads like English.
   • Support: the loop repeats once per item.  • Core: what `item` is each time.  • Challenge: why `for` is tidier than `while` for a list.
2. (5–20) I-do → we-do — three for-loop patterns: print every item · count matches · collect matches into a new list. Card-sort on the activity worksheet: which lines belong to the counting pattern vs the collecting pattern.
3. (20–30) We-do — lipogram (embedded Gadsby cover): loop over every word and check `if "e" in word`. Real hook: a 50,000-word novel with no letter “e”.
4. (30–45) You-do — English words (embedded screenshot): count words of a given length, then collect the words that contain a piece of text.
   • Support: pick the loop header.  • Core: fill the gap; print every word. Likely error: indenting the check outside the loop → fix-words “the work happens inside the loop, one indent in.” Keep a screenshot.
5. (45–53) Challenge — heartbeats (embedded ECG plot): loop over real heart data and count beats by spotting zero crossings (fast finishers).
6. (53–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; name the pattern used (print/count/collect). TA notes who can read a for loop aloud.
Differentiation on the SAME task; movement breaks are routine; no flashing/sound.

lessons.title: Make a thing — sums, strings and mini-projects
lessons.objectives:
I can build up a sum or count inside a loop
I can use a for loop over the characters of a string
I can combine selection, iteration and list and string operations
I can choose and start a mini-project
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: accumulate · sum · for · string · character. TA: editor open; pairs swap roles. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — sums on the starter worksheet: predict the output of repeated `total = total + number`. Walk it through on the board, writing `total` each step.
   • Support: `+ 4` makes total 4 bigger.  • Core: what `total = total + number` does.  • Challenge: why total needs a start value.
2. (8–15) We-do — sums in a for-loop: put `total = total + number` inside a `for` loop.
   • Support: pick the accumulator line.  • Core: fill the gap. Likely error: resetting total inside the loop → fix-words “set the total ONCE, before the loop.”
3. (15–28) You-do — count the vowels (embedded screenshot). Parsons task on the activity worksheet: order the jumbled lines so the program counts vowels in a sentence. Watch the double indent (inside the loop AND the if).
4. (28–50) Challenge — make a thing: each pupil chooses ONE mini-project (planetary quiz / password checker / Caesar cipher / ISBN checker), writes a one-line plan and the first lines. Everyone makes something; the project sets the ceiling. Keep a screenshot.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-confidence (embedded rating table): rate confidence with for-on-strings and building sums on the worksheet sliders. TA notes low ratings for a re-teach.
Differentiation on the SAME task; calm, one project each; movement breaks are routine; no flashing/sound.

lessons.title: Wrap up — finish, show and reflect
lessons.objectives:
I can finish and show my mini-project
I can read list and string code and predict its output
I can complete a loop that goes through a sequence
I can reflect on what I have learned this unit
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: sequence · list · string · selection · iteration. TA: editor open. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud. Calm wrap-up lesson.
1. (0–5) Starter — “what do you remember?” on the starter worksheet: a multi-select of which operations work on a list (append/index/count, not print).
   • Support: a list and a string are both sequences.  • Core: one thing you can do to both.  • Challenge: one way they differ.
2. (5–20) Mini-project completion: quiet working time to finish last lesson’s project, or explore a second. Praise working programs, however small.
3. (20–45) Show what you know — the activity worksheet is the assessment. Read short programs about days, planets and the alphabet.
   • Support: match the string operations.  • Core: fill the loop gap; what `index` returns. Likely error: off-by-one in `index < 3` for four rocky planets → fix-words “four planets means indices 0,1,2,3, so use `< 4`.”  • Challenge: read the planets output and fix the condition. Everyone does the Parsons (the alphabet-position program) — it pulls input, a for-loop over a string, and `index` together.
4. (45–53) A peek ahead — images are sequences too: a colour-filter program loops over pixels just like a list (optional showcase for fast finishers; no new task).
5. (53–55) Reflection — write one thing learned and rate confidence with lists and strings (slider). Sticky notes work too: one learned, one surprise, one to improve.
Differentiation on the SAME task; movement breaks are routine; no flashing/sound.
